content: **Golden and Silver Rules**
**Golden Rule**
Card text supersedes rules text. Whenever a card fundamentally contradicts the rules, the card's indication is what is true.
**Silver Rule**
Card text uses different terminology than rules. Card text should be interpreted according to these rules, not as though it were text within these rules.

**Card,** when written in card effects, is shorthand for "**Main Deck** card." Runes, legends, and battlefields are _not_ considered cards when executing the abilities and effects of game objects. They are considered cards for the purposes of these rules.
Cards refer to themselves in the first person.
* Units and legends say "I," "me," etc.
* Gear and spells say "this."
* Battlefields say "here."
* Cards may refer to themselves by their name for clarity. This is shorthand for the above terms.
“Can’t beats Can”
Cards that forbid actions or effects, as a broad method of determination, supersede cards that allow or permit that same action or effect.
If a card specifies that an action can “only” be performed under certain circumstances, it cannot be performed under any other circumstances.
When executing card text, do as much as you can, ignoring impossible instructions.
If all of a card’s instructions are impossible, it is still played and resolved, but nothing happens.
Cards a player owns may never be placed into a non-Board zone belonging to another player.
Non-Board zones corresponding to a player include Main Deck, Rune Deck, Trash, Hand, Chosen Champion zone, and Banishment.
If a card would enter such a zone, it goes to its owner’s corresponding zone instead.
**Game Concepts**
**Deck Construction**
_Riftbound_ is a Trading Card Game where a player must provide their own cards to play against other players.

To play _Riftbound_, a player must have a **Main Deck,** a **Rune Deck,** a **Champion Legend**, and a number of **Battlefields** determined by the **Mode of Play.** These are collectively referred to as a player’s deck.
1 **Champion Legend**
This is placed in the **Legend Zone** at the start of the game.
This will dictate the **Domain Identity** of the deck.
Cards included in your deck must abide by your **Domain Identity.**
Your deck’s **Domain Identity** is dictated by the domains of your **Champion Legend.** In the default card frame, these domains appear as symbols in the legend’s upper left corner.
If a card has a single **Domain**, then that card is permitted in the **Domain Identity** that corresponds to the same **Domain.**
If a card has more than one **Domain**, then that card is permitted _only_ in a **Domain** **Identity** that contains all of the indicated **Domains** on that card.

Some **Game Effects** may allow a player to add cards to their deck irrespective of those cards’ **Domains.** Those cards are considered part of their deck’s **Domain** **Identity,** regardless of their **Domain.**
**A Main Deck of at least 40 cards:** A Chosen Champion Unit, as well as Units, Gear, and Spells
**Chosen Champion**
This will be placed in the **Champion Zone** at the start of the game.

Must be a champion unit with a champion tag that matches the tag on your **Champion Legend.**
_Example:_ Loose Cannon has the tag _Jinx_. Therefore, a player could choose Jinx, Rebel or Jinx, Demolitionist as their Chosen Champion, because they also have the tag _Jinx_.
_Example:_ Tibbers has the tag _Annie_, but it is a signature unit, not a champion unit. It cannot be your Chosen Champion, even if your Champion Legend has the tag _Annie_.

A player’s **Chosen Champion** is both the specific card chosen for this slot during Deck Building and also any **Champion Unit** with the same name as the specific card selected for this specific slot during the course of play.
_Example:_ A player chose Jinx, Rebel as their Chosen Champion. One copy of Jinx, Rebel starts the game in their Chosen Champion zone. Any additional copies of Jinx, Rebel in their Main Deck, their hand, their trash, or on the board also count as their Chosen Champion for any game rules and effects that care about Chosen Champion status during the game.
Your **Main Deck** can include up to 3 copies of the same named card.

This includes your **Chosen Champion.**
_Example:_ A deck could include Volibear, Furious as its Chosen Champion and still include 2 more copies of Volibear, Furious in their Main Deck.

Cards have different names even if they represent the same character. _Example:_ A deck could include 3 copies of Yasuo, Remorseful and 3 copies of Yasuo, Windrider, because they have different names.
Subject to **Domain Identity.**
Your deck may only contain 3 _total_ **Signature** cards that have the same Champion tag as your **Champion Legend.**
Regardless of name, a deck may only contain a sum total of 3 Signature cards.
All of the Signature cards must have the Champion tag that corresponds to the **Champion Legend** of the deck.
Signature cards are not Champion units and cannot be placed in the **Champion** **Zone**.
Subject to card legality of the **Format** being played.
**Rune Deck**
12 **Rune Cards**
Cards in the **Rune Deck** must be of the **Domain Identity** of your **Champion** **Legend.**
Must be shuffled and kept separate from the **Main Deck.**
**Battlefields**
The number will be dictated by your **Mode of Play.**
Subject to **Domain Identity** if applicable.
Cannot include more than one of a **Battlefield** of the same name when there are more than one required for the deck.
**Setup**
Spaces
The **Play Area** is a collection of logical **Zones** that are defined by their behaviors and relationships to **Players.** These **Zones** are grouped into **The Board** and **Non-Board Zones.**
**The Board**
**Bases**
Each player has their own **Base.**

Each Base is a **Location.**
_See_ ***rule 197. Locations*** _for more information._
**Permanents** and **Runes** controlled by a player reside in that player's **Base.**
**Permanents** attached to **Permanents** controlled by a player may also be found in that player’s **Base,** regardless of their controller.
**Permanents** and **Runes** in **Bases** are **Public Information.**
**Battlefield Zone**
The **Battlefield Zone** houses multiple **Battlefields.**

Each **Battlefield** is individually a **Location.**
_See_ ***rule 197. Locations*** _for more information._
**Battlefields** and permanents at **Battlefields** are **Public Information.**
**Facedown Zones**
Each Battlefield is associated with a sub-zone called a **Facedown Zone**, a single logical space that a card can be put facedown in through various **Game Effects.**
Each **Facedown Zone** has a maximum occupancy of one card.
The maximum occupancy of a **Facedown Zone** can increase or decrease.

If the maximum occupancy of a **Facedown Zone** decreases to a number less than the number of cards currently located there, the controller of that **Facedown Zone** must put a number of cards equal to the difference in the trash from the **Facedown Zone.**
Cards can only be placed in or occupy the **Facedown Zone** if the controller of the card also controls the associated **Battlefield.**

If a player loses **Control** of a **Battlefield**, any cards in the **Facedown Zone** associated with that **Battlefield** are removed during the next **Cleanup.**
_See_ ***rule 318. Cleanups*** _for more information._
_See_ ***rule 421. Hide*** _for more information._
Facedown Zones are not locations.
Facedown Zones are **Public Zones,** though facedown cards located there are **Private.**
**Legend Zone**
The space for each player's **Champion Legend.**
This is not a location.

The **Champion Legend** here is a **Game Object.**
_See_ ***rule 119. Game Objects*** _for more information._
The **Champion Legend** cannot be removed, moved, or displaced from this zone.
Some effects may add a legend card or token to the **Legend Zone.** These legends are not Champion Legends.
Non-Champion legends may be removed from the Legend Zone. They cannot exist in any zone other than the Legend Zone or **Banishment.**
**Non-Board Zones**
**The Chain**
Cards and abilities are placed here as part of the process of being **played.** _See_ ***rule 327. Chains*** _for more information._
Cards and abilities on the **Chain** are **Public Information.**
**Trashes**
Each player has their own **Trash.**
This space is where cards are placed when they are killed or discarded, when they finish being executed as spells, when they are moved to the trash as an effect, etc.
Cards in each player’s **Trash** are **unordered.** Their sequence does not matter, and they may be reorganized.

Cards in a player's **Trash** are **Public** **Information.**
_See_ ***rule 128. Privacy*** _for more information._
**Champion Zones**
Each player has their own **Champion Zone.**
The space each player puts their **Chosen Champion** at the start of the game.
The **Chosen Champion** cannot be returned to this zone by normal means.
If a **Chosen Champion** is instructed to be returned to this zone, it can only do so if there is not a card already in this zone.

The **Chosen Champion** can be played from here as normal, following the rules of **Playing a** **Card.**
_See_ ***rule 349. Playing Cards*** _for more information._
Cards in a player’s **Champion Zone** are **Public Information.**
**Main Deck Zones**
Each player has their own **Main Deck Zone.**
The space for the player's **Main Deck.**
Primarily houses the face-down **Main Deck** during gameplay.
The order of cards in the **Main Deck** is **Secret Information** during the course of play. _See_ ***rule 128. Privacy*** _for more information._
**Rune Deck Zones**
Each player has their own **Rune Deck Zone.**
The space for the player's **Rune Deck.**
Primarily houses the face-down runes during gameplay.
The order of runes in the **Rune Deck** is **Secret Information** during the course of play. _See_ ***rule 128. Privacy*** _for more information._
**Banishments**
Each player has their own **Banishment.**
This space is where cards are placed when they are banished by a spell or effect.
Represents cards that have been removed from play in a more difficult-to-recover way, ***or*** a temporary space to hold cards while effects are being processed.
The cards in the banishment are **unordered.** Their sequence does not matter, and they may be reorganized.

The cards in any player's **Banishment** are Public **Information.**
_See_ ***rule 128. Privacy*** _for more information._
**The Hand**
Each player has their own **Hand.**
This is the location cards go when **Drawn.**
The cards in a player's hand are **Private Information.**
The cards in a player’s hand are **Unordered.** Their sequence does not matter and they may be reorganized.
The number of cards in a player's hand is **Public Information.**
The hand can be targeted as a zone, and cards there can be affected by spells and effects when specified.
All **Game Objects** in the collective **Play Areas** are **Public Information.**
Any player may view, or be provided with the details of the face-up information printed on the cards in the **Play Area.**

The **State** of all **Game Objects** in the **Play Area** is also **Public Information.**
_Examples:_
If a **Unit** is **Buffed**
If a **Gear** is **Exhausted**
Etc.
Setup Process
Each player separates their **Champion Legend** and places it in the **Legend Zone.**
Each player separates their **Chosen Champion** and places it in the **Champion Zone.**
Each player sets aside their **Battlefields.**
Your **Mode of Play** will dictate how these are used and placed into the **Battlefield Zone.** _See_ ***rule 481. Modes of Play*** _for more information._
Each player shuffles their **Main** and **Rune Decks**, separately, then places them into their respective Zones.
The **Main Deck** is placed in the **Main Deck Zone.**
The **Rune Deck** is placed in the **Rune Deck Zone.**
Determine **Turn Order** using any fair random method agreed on by all players.
**Turn Order** is established as a repeating set of the players.
The **Mode of Play** will specify how to determine a **First Player.**
If not otherwise specified, **Seating** then determines the sequence, play proceeding clockwise from the **First Player.**
The **First Player** is the player who becomes the **Turn Player** first.
The **Turn Order** generates a looping queue of turns, starting with the First Player and repeating until the game ends.

See the selected **Mode of Play** for any adjustments to each player's first turn.
_See_ ***rule 481. Modes of Play*** _for more information._
Players each draw 4.
In turn order, players perform their **Mulligan.**
A player may choose up to two cards in their hand. They set those cards aside.
Then, that player draws as many cards as they set aside.

Finally, that player **Recycles** the cards that were set aside.
_See_ ***rule 416. Recycle*** _for more information._
Begin play with the **First Player** taking their turn.
**Game Objects**
A **Game Object** is any game piece that can produce one or more **Game Effects** or grant prerequisites for players to take **Game Actions.**
A **Game Object** does not include nor preclude any inherent properties beyond the ability to produce, or act as the prerequisite for, **Game Effects** and **Game Actions.**
A **Game Object** can be a literal object or a logical object.

**Game Objects** include, but are not limited to, the following:
Main Deck cards in any zone
Runes in any zone
Legends
Battlefields
Tokens in any zone
Abilities of any type on the chain
Counters and other status markers
A **Game Object** that changes zones to or from a **Non-Board Zone** becomes a new object for the purposes of tracking that object.

Whenever a **Game Object** changes zones to or from a **Non-Board Zone**, all **Temporary Modifications** of all kinds cease to be tracked on it in all capacities.
_Examples:_
Damage is cleared.
Counters are removed.
Granted **Keywords** are no longer granted.
Statuses are cleared.

A **Game Object** can have any of the following statuses (non-exhaustive):
Attached, Attacking, Buffed, Banished, Controlled, Defending, Empowered, Equipped, Exhausted, Facedown, Readied, Replaced, Revealed, Stunned, and any applied Layer alternations.
**Cards**
_Riftbound_ games are composed of players playing the cards in their respective decks.
**Ownership**

For gameplay purposes, a card's **Owner** is the player who brought it into the game, either as their **Champion Legend**, one of their **Battlefields**, as part of their **Main Deck** or **Rune Deck,** or **Created** during the process of play. The legal owner of a card doesn't matter during gameplay. _Example:_ Bo didn't bring a deck, so Alice loans him a deck. During the game, Bo is considered the owner of those cards, even though legally they belong to Alice.
**Privacy**
A card will always exist in one of the many **Zones** described during **Setup.**
A card will have a different level of **Privacy** based on its state or where it resides at a given time.
Unless specified otherwise by the state of the card, a card’s **Privacy** is the same as the zone it is located in.
**Secret:** This level of Privacy indicates that no player may read or look at the face of the card.

**Private:** This level of Privacy indicates that only the controller of a card on the board or the owner of a card in any other zone may read or look at the face of the card.
_Example:_ Cards in a player's hand are owned by that player. Only that player may read or look at their faces.
_Example:_ If a player controls a facedown card at a battlefield, that player and only that player may read or look at that card's face, regardless of who owns that card.
**Public:** This level of Privacy indicates that any player may read or look at the face of the card.

A player cannot be compelled to perform an action on cards whose privacy is secret or private if that action specifies a type or quality of card.
_Example:_ A card effect reads, “Play a unit from your hand, ignoring its costs.” Although the effect does not say “may”, the player can choose not to perform the action even if they have a unit in their hand, because the effect specifies a card type.

If an effect would compel a player to perform an action on cards whose privacy is secret or private, and if that effect specifies a type or quality of card, the player in question may choose to ignore that instruction. Ignored instructions are deemed impossible.
**Back Side**
The back side of a card is the side with the **Riftbound** logo.
There is a unique pattern of back side for each of the three categories of card: **Main Deck** cards, **Rune** **Deck** cards, and **Battlefields** and **Champion Legends.**
The back side of a card is presented to conceal information for **Private** and **Secret** information cards. For example, cards in the **Main Deck** and cards in hand have their back side presented.
The term **facedown** is used to describe the state during regular play of having a card's back side presented on the Board. A facedown card’s front side is considered **Private Information.**
**Front Side**
The front side of a card is the opposite of its back.
This is also referred to as a **front face.**
This can be identified in situations where the back is not easily discernible by the presence of the **Play** **Cost** in the upper left corner of the card.
Cards, when played, are played with their front face presented.
The term **face up** is used to describe the state during regular play of having a card's front face presented

Cards are considered to be **Public Information** while in this state, even if obscured by other cards in a pile. _Example:_ The Trash is **Public Information** because all cards there have their front face presented even while stacked.
**Cost**
**Main Deck** cards have a **Cost**, listed in the upper left corner of the front face of the card. _See_ ***rule 165. Rune Pools*** _for more information._
**Energy** Cost
The numeral listed in the **Cost** element in the upper left corner of the card.
**Power** cost
The symbols, listed vertically, in the **Cost** element in the upper left corner of the card.
This section may not be present on every card.
**Name**
Each card has a name that identifies it uniquely.
This is usually located in the middle of the card.

Cards that are printed in different languages but represent the same card are considered to have the same name for the purposes of deckbuilding and gameplay.
_Example:_ Chemtech Enforcer in English and its counterpart in Chinese are considered the same card, despite the **Name** element on the card reading differently.

Some cards have both a short name and a subtitle. For all purposes, including rules and deckbuilding, such a card’s name is “[Short Name], [Subtitle]”.
_Example:_ Kai’Sa, Evolutionary and Kai’Sa, Survivor both have the short name Kai’Sa, but they have different names. You can include 3 of each in your deck under normal deckbuilding rules. If one of them is your Chosen Champion, the other is not.
**Category**
A card can have one or more **Categories** and **Sub-Categories** based on the properties of its front and back sides.
These **Categories** and **Sub-Categories** dictate the behaviors of the card during play.

Spells and other effects can refer to categories, sub-categories, supertypes, card types, tags, and other characteristics inclusively or exclusively.
_Example:_ A "non-unit card" is any card that is not a unit.
_Example:_ A "unit" is any game object that is a unit, regardless of any other categories it belongs to.
**Main Deck Cards** begin the game in the **Main Deck** or (in the case of a **Chosen Champion**) the **Champion Zone.**
**Permanents**
An umbrella sub-category encompassing **Main Deck** Game Objects that remain on the board after being played.
**Unit** and **Gear** are permanent types.
**Spells**
A sub-category encompassing Main Deck cards with the spell type, which do not remain on the board after being played.
**Rune Deck Cards** begin the game in the **Rune Deck.**
**Runes**

A sub-category encompassing cards with the rune type. These are **channeled** rather than played. They remain on the board after being channeled, but they are _not_ permanents, as they are not **Main Deck** cards.
**Other Cards** are not part of either the **Main** or **Rune Decks** and begin the game in a zone determined by their type.
**Battlefields**
A sub-category encompassing cards with the battlefield type. They are not played or channeled. They start the game on the board.
**Legends**
A sub-category encompassing cards with the legend type. They are not played or channeled. They start the game in the Legend Zone and cannot typically leave it during play.
**Supertypes** are **Categories** that may apply to game objects of multiple types. They are listed before a card’s type.

**Champion** is a supertype that applies exclusively to units. It impacts the rules for deckbuilding.
_See_ ***rule 103.2.a. Chosen Champion*** _for more information._

**Signature** is a supertype that may apply to game objects of any card type. It impacts the rules for deckbuilding.
_See_ ***rule 103.2.d.*** _for more information about_ ***Signature cards.***
**Tags** are **Categories** that may apply to game objects of multiple types. They are listed after a card’s type.
**Tags** have no innate rules meaning, but may be referenced by game rules and card effects.
**Tags** used to link **Legends**, **Champion Units**, and **Signature** cards are known as **Champion** **Tags.**
**Domain**
Most cards belong to one or more of six **Domains**, identified by one or more symbols in the lower right corner.
Each Domain has an associated color, a unique symbol, and a shorthand used to represent that symbol in written text.
**Fury** is associated with the color red and represented by a circular symbol with three projecting points. Its shorthand is [R].
**Calm** is associated with the color green and represented by a symbol resembling a leaf. Its shorthand is [G].
**Mind** is associated with the color blue and represented by a symbol resembling a sun and moon. Its shorthand is [B].
**Body** is associated with the color orange and represented by a blocky diamond-shaped symbol. Its shorthand is [O].
**Chaos** is associated with the color purple and represented by a hexagonal symbol with swirls emanating from its center. Its shorthand is [P].
**Order** is associated with the color yellow and represented by an angular winged symbol. Its shorthand is [Y].
**Rules Text**
All cards have a section that describes how they affect the game. This section is known as the **Rules** **Text** of the card.
This section may contain:
**Abilities**
Abilities are discrete pieces of text that describe what a card may or must do. _See_ ***rule 360. Abilities*** _for more information._
They may appear on any card.
**Instructions**
Instructions are a type of text that appears on spells and within abilities.
They describe actions to be performed by one or more players.

Instructions will self-describe when they are to be executed. If there is no timing described, they will execute during resolution.
_Example:_ An instruction says “as you play me, kill a unit as an additional cost.” That instruction will execute as the card is being played. If the card is given Repeat and the Repeat cost is paid, this instruction will not execute because the Repeat execution does not happen until resolution of the spell, after this instruction has executed.
Instructions can usually be recognized by the way they are written. An instruction is written in the imperative mood, using the base form of a verb and omitting the subject unless necessary.
A single instruction is made up of a game action and its complement.

The complement of a game action is made up of the players or **Game** **Objects** that perform that game action, that that game action is performed on, the condition under which it is performed, and the duration for its effect to last. Some game actions omit a complement entirely. _Example:_ Void Seeker is made up of two instructions. The first is “deal 4 to a unit at a battlefield.” Here “deal 4” is the game action, while “a unit at a battlefield,” is its complement. The second instruction, “draw 1,” has no complement.
_Example:_ Teemo, Strategist’s defend trigger reads “When I defend, choose an enemy unit here and reveal the top 5 cards of your Main Deck. Deal 1 to that unit for each card with Hidden revealed this way, then recycle the revealed cards.” There are four instructions here: “choose an enemy unit here,” “reveal the top 5 cards of your Main Deck,” “deal 1 to that unit for each card with Hidden revealed this way,” and “recycle the revealed cards.”

Instructions do not include trigger conditions or the events or actions that a replacement effect defines as being eligible to be replaced.
_Example:_ An ability reads “When you play me, draw 1.” The trigger condition for this ability is not an instruction. “Draw 1,” is the only instruction included in the ability.
_Example:_ An ability reads “If a unit would die, banish it instead.” The text “If a unit would die,” is not an instruction of the ability.
_Example:_ An ability reads “As I’m revealed from your Main Deck, Add [2].” The text “As I’m revealed from your Main Deck,” is not an instruction of the ability.
**Keywords**
Keywords are short words or phrases that represent longer abilities or instructions. _See_ ***rule 800. Keywords*** _for more information._
Keywords may or may not have reminder text.
**Reminder Text**
Reminder text appears in italics and parentheses.
Its purpose is to summarize relevant rules, including keywords and other rules.
The presence, absence, or exact wording of reminder text has no effect on game function.
**Symbols**
Rules text sometimes contains symbols that express game concepts. Each symbol also has a shorthand used to represent it in written text.

The cost of **Exhausting** a **Permanent** is represented by the “exhaust symbol,” which resembles a card turning sideways. Its shorthand is [E]. (Some previous rules information has given its shorthand as [T].)
**Might** is represented by the “Might symbol,” which resembles a sword and shield. Its shorthand is [M]. (Some previous rules information has given its shorthand as [S].)

**Power** of a specific **Domain** is represented by its domain symbol and the corresponding shorthand.
_See_ ***rule 134. Domain*** _for more information._
Power of any **Domain** is represented by a swirling rainbow symbol. Its shorthand is [A].
When required as a cost, [A] can be paid by **Power** of any **Domain**.
When **Added** to a player’s **Rune Pool,** [A] can be spent to pay a **Power** cost of any **Domain.**
Power of a domain corresponding to a card’s own **Domain** is not represented by a symbol, but appears in these rules similarly to other symbols. Its shorthand is [C].
When a [C] shorthand is printed on a card as reminder text, it assumes that card’s usual **Domain**, but it still means “one Power of this card’s **Domain**.”
A [C] shorthand on a card with no **Domain** is processed as [A] instead.

A [C] shorthand on a card with multiple **Domains** is processed as any power of that card’s **Domains.**
_Example:_ Defiant Dance is a Calm and Chaos spell. Its cost is [1][C]. The power component of that cost can be paid with either [G] or [P].
_Example:_ Tibbers is a Fury and Chaos Unit. If Tibbers is granted the Accelerate keyword, the power component of the optional additional cost can be paid with either [R] or [P].
A keyword associated with an ability is indicated by the [>] symbol.

This could be a dependent keyword like [Legion][>] or [Level][>], a triggered ability with a keyworded trigger condition like [Deathknell] or it could be a permissive keyword associated with an ability like [Action][>] or [Reaction][>].

Whatever instruction or ability comes after the [>] is modified by the keyword that comes before the [>]. It will either be inactive, in the case of a dependent keyword; the trigger effect, in the case of a keyword triggered ability; or it will receive the permission granted by the keyword in the case of a permissive keyword.
Rules text can be blank.

A card’s printed **Rules Text** is **Inactive** while that card is **Attached** to another card. _See_ ***rule 716. Attachment*** _for more information._
_See_ ***rule 720. Inactive*** _for more information._
The presence of text, rules, **Keywords**, and other effects can still be referenced and detected by other game effects, even while **Rules Text** is **Inactive.**
Any granted or appended **Rules Text** on a card is still **Active** even if that card is **Attached** to another card.
**Effect Text**
Some cards have a separate section of text below the **Rules Text**. This is referred to as the **Effect Text**.
**Effect Text** can contain additional **Abilities.**
**Effect Text** can be blank.

**Effect Text** is **inactive** unless the card with the **Effect Text** is **Attached** to another card. _See_ ***rule 716. Attachment*** _for more information._
_See_ ***rule 720. Inactive*** _for more information._
The abilities in the **Effect Text** section of a card are appended to the **Rules Text** of the card to which the card with the **Effect Text** is **Attached.**

**Effect Text** may refer to “this” or to the name of the **Attached** game object that appended the **Effect Text.** Doing so refers to the **Attached** game object and not the **Top-Most Card,** even if the **Top-Most Card** shares a name with the **Attached** game object. _Example:_ Guardian Angel’s effect text reads “If I would die, kill Guardian Angel instead. Heal me, exhaust me, and recall me.”
_Example:_ Brutalizer’s effect text reads “If this was attached to me this turn, I have an additional +2 [M].” The “this” in the effect text refers to the attached Brutalizer.
**Might Bonus**
Some cards have a **Might Bonus** in their lower right corner, expressed as an operator plus an integer.
A card’s **Might Bonus** can be **+0.**

A card’s **Might Bonus** modulates the **Might** of the card to which the card that has the **Might Bonus** is **Attached**.
_See_ ***rule 716. Attachment*** _for more information._
The **Might Bonus** is applied while **Attached** and stops applying as soon as the card with the **Might Bonus** is no longer **Attached.**
If the card to which a card with a **Might Bonus** is **Attached** has no **Might** value, the **Might** **Bonus** is ignored.
**Flavor Text**

Flavor text is text that appears at the bottom of some cards to provide aesthetic enjoyment. It conveys no gameplay information.
_Example:_ The card Void Seeker features a quote from the character Kai'Sa. It does not have the Kai'Sa tag and has no gameplay connection to the Champion unit Kai'Sa.
Flavor text is located at the bottom of the section provided for rules text, usually in a shaded bar. It appears in italics.
**Illustration**
Each card has an illustration. It conveys no gameplay information.
**Units**
**Unit is**:
A **Game Object**
While on **the Board**:
**Units** are at one of several **Locations** while on **the Board**: a **Battlefield** or their **Base.**
**Units** and their details are Public Information while on **the Board.**
**Units** can be chosen, affected, or manipulated by spells, affects, or game actions that specify **Units.**

**Units** can be **Killed.**
_See_ ***rule 428. Kill*** _for more information._
While in the **Trash:**
**Units** are treated as **Cards**, similar to when in the **Hand.**
They retain the properties of being a **Unit**, but are not on **the Board** and thus cannot take actions or be affected by spells, abilities, or game actions that target **Units** on **the Board.**
Units can be affected by spells and game effects that target **Units** in the **Trash.**
A Card Type
This is a unique identifier that some spells or abilities will use to restrict what they can choose or affect.
The card type is relevant in all zones.
**Damage** is a marked value that is applied to **Units.**
**Damage** is not a **Game Object.**
**Damage** is a value tracked per-Unit.
**Damage** is marked on Units by players.
The player responsible for the **Deal** action that caused the **Damage** to be marked is the player who marked that **Damage.**

**Game Effects** may refer to that player’s **Damage.** This means the **Damage** marked by that player.
_Example:_ A unit reads in part “Your damage can’t be prevented.” This refers to the damage marked by that player.

**Damage** tracks how close a **Unit** is to being **Killed.**
_See_ ***rule 428. Kill*** _for more information._
**Lethal Damage** is the amount of marked **Damage** that will cause a unit to die in a cleanup.

**Lethal Damage** for a Unit is a non-zero amount greater than or equal to that Unit’s **Might.** _Example:_ A unit has 5 [M] and 3 damage marked on it. Frigid Touch is played targeting that unit. When it resolves, the unit’s Might becomes 3, and it will have lethal damage marked on it.
_Example:_ A unit has 0 [M]. In order to have lethal damage marked on it, it must have at least 1 damage marked on it.

Some effects may alter this amount. These effects will refer to the amount of damage needed to kill a unit.
_Example:_ Elder Dragon’s passive ability reads “Any amount of your damage is enough to kill enemy units.” This alters the **Lethal Damage** value for enemy units that have damage marked by you.

**Damage** can be **Healed.**
_See_ ***rule 418. Heal*** _for more information_.
**Units** have multiple **Intrinsic Properties** unique to them:
**Tag:** A **Unit** has zero or more **Tags** representing one or more champions, regions, factions, or species it belongs to.
These have no intrinsic rules or behaviors by themselves.
Spells, abilities, and game actions can reference these types as part of their execution.
**Might**: The combat statistic of a **Unit**. Used to determine a **Unit's** contribution to **Combat**, as well as when it is **Killed** by damaging effects.
If a **Unit** ever has nonzero damage marked on it equalling or exceeding its **Might**, it is **Killed.**

If a unit's Might is ever less than 0, it is treated as 0 when referenced by spells and abilities, and when summing Might to be assigned as damage in the **Combat Damage Step**. _See_ ***rule 465. The Combat Damage Step*** _for more information._
Although the unit’s Might is treated as 0, it is not 0. Effects that calculate Might increases and decreases use the actual value of the unit’s Might.
**Units** can have damage marked on them.
When spells, abilities, or other game effects deal damage, **Units** mark that damage on them temporarily. This can be tracked with coins, dice, or other markers, or by memory.
**Damage** is **Healed** from **Units** at two specific times:

At the end of each player's turn.
_See_ ***rule 317.2. Ending Phase*** _for more information._

During a **Combat Cleanup.**
_See_ ***rule 466.1.*** _for more information about_ ***Combat Cleanups.***
**Units** enter **the Board** **exhausted.**

This can be altered by **Accelerate** or similar game effects.
_See_ ***rule 805. Accelerate*** _for more information._
**Units** have the **Inherent Ability** to perform a **Standard Move.**
This action is limited in when it can be performed.
This action can be done any time during a player's **Main Phase.**
This action cannot be performed during a **Closed State**.
This action cannot be performed during a **Showdown** or **Combat.**
**Exhausting** the **Unit** is the **Cost** for this action.
Players may perform multiple **Units'** standard move simultaneously. This is treated as one game action performed on multiple **Units.**
When a **Move** like this is declared by a player, the units' **Destination** must be the same.
When a **Move** like this is declared by a player, the **Origins** do not need to be the same.
The **Costs** of **Exhausting** the **Units** are also paid **Simultaneously.**
The Destinations where **Units** can **Move** to with their **Standard Move** are restricted:
**Units** may move from their **Base** to a **Battlefield.**

**Units** ***cannot*** **Move** to a **Battlefield** that already has units from 2 other players present, or where a **Combat** is ongoing that has 2 other players as participants. _See_ ***rule 447.2.*** _For more information on valid destinations for movement._
**Units** may move from a **Battlefield** to their **Base.**
**Ganking** is a unique ability that affects a **Unit's** **Standard Move**

**Units** with **Ganking** may use their **Standard Move** to **Move** from **Battlefield** to **Battlefield.**
_See_ ***rule 810. Ganking*** _for more information._
**Units** may have **Activated** **Abilities.**

**Activated Abilities** are **Game Effects** that are written as **Costs** followed by a ":", and then succeeded by an effect.
_See_ ***rule 376. Activated Abilities*** _for more information._
The **Activated Ability** of **Units** may be executed at any time during the controlling player's **Main** **Phase** during an **Open State**, and not during a **Showdown.**

This follows the same process as playing a card.
_See_ ***rule 349. Playing Cards*** _for more information._
This behaves, once activated, like a spell without an associated card.
**Units** have a **Location.**

A **Unit’s** **Location** is the **Base** or **Battlefield** it currently occupies.
_See_ ***rule 197. Locations*** _for more information._
**Gear**
**Gear** are:
A **Game Object**
While on **the Board.**
**Gear** can only be played to a player's **Base** unless an effect specifies otherwise.
**Gear** and their details are Public Information while on **the Board.**
**Gear** can be chosen, affected, or manipulated by spells, effects, or game actions that specify **Gear.**

**Gear** can be **Killed.**
_See_ ***rule 428. Kill*** _for more information._
While in the **Trash**
**Gear** are treated as **Cards** before **Game Objects**, similar to the **Hand.**
They retain the properties of being **Gear**, but are not on **the Board** and thus cannot take actions or be affected by spells, abilities, or game actions that affect **Gear** on **the Board.**
**Gear** can be affected by spells and game effects that affect **Gear** in the **Trash.**
A Card Type
This is a unique identifier that some spells or abilities will use to restrict what they can choose or affect.
The card type is relevant in all zones.
**Gear** have several **Intrinsic Properties** unique to them.
**Gear** enter play **Ready.**
**Gear** can only be played to a player's **Base** unless an effect specifies otherwise.

If an unattached non-**Unit** **Gear** is at a **Battlefield** for any reason during a cleanup, then it is recalled to its controller's **Base** as a corrective action.
_See_ ***rule 454. Recalls*** _for more information._
_See_ ***rule 318. Cleanups*** _for more information._
**Gear** can have the **Equipment** tag.
These **Gear** are referred to as **Equipment.**

**Equipment** have effect text and a Might bonus.
_See_ ***rule 136. Effect Text*** _for more information on effect text._
_See_ ***rule 137. Might Bonus*** _for more information on Might bonuses._

**Equipment** can have the **Equip** or **Quick-Draw** keywords.
_See_ ***rule 818. Equip*** _for more information on the Equip keyword._
_See_ ***rule 819. Quick-Draw*** _for more information on the Quick-Draw keyword._
**Equipment** are still **Gear** and have the same intrinsic properties and rules that **Gear** do.
**Equipment** and whether a **Gear** has the **Equipment** tag is a characteristic of the **Gear** may be checked or referenced by other **Game Effects.**
**Gear** may have **Activated** **Abilities.**

**Activated Abilities** are **Game Effects** that are written as **Costs** followed by a ":", and then succeeded by an effect.
_See_ ***rule 376. Activated Abilities*** _for more information._
The **Activated Ability** of **Gear** may be executed at any time during the controlling player's **Main** **Phase** during an **Open State**, and not during a **Showdown.**

This follows the same process as playing a card.
_See_ ***rule 349. Playing Cards*** _for more information._
This behaves, once activated, like a spell without an associated card.
**Gear** have a **Location.**

A **Gear’s Location** is the **Base** or **Battlefield** it currently occupies.
_See rule 197. Locations for more information._

**Non-Unit Gear** cannot normally become located at a **Battlefield** unless by some special means. _Example:_ Gear are played to Base unless an effect specifies otherwise. A gear played from Facedown, which is specified to be played to the Battlefield it was played from, will enter at that Battlefield. Gear can also become located at a Battlefield if they are attached to a unit who becomes located at a Battlefield. A Gear that is a Unit can move to a Battlefield. _See_ ***rule 811. Hidden*** _for more information._
_See_ ***rule 716. Attachment*** _for more information._
**Spells**
Spell is a card type.
A spell can be played during an **Open State** outside of **Showdowns** on its controller's turn.
A spell is controlled by the player who played it.
A spell creates a game effect according to its instructions and is then placed in the **Trash** of the player who owns it.
When a spell is successfully played, a player executes the rules text of the spell. This is called **Resolving** the spell.
Spells have their rules text executed from top to bottom when they are **Resolved.**

If a later part of a spell applies a **Replacement Effect** that alters earlier parts of the spell, apply those replacement effects as appropriate. _Example:_ A spell says "Choose a unit. Kill it the next time it takes damage this turn." and "[Legion] — Kill it now instead. _(Get the effect if you've played another card_ _this turn.)_" If the Legion condition is satisfied, the unit is killed immediately and the instruction to kill it the next time it takes damage is ignored, even if the unit remains on the board somehow. _See_ ***rule 367. Replacement Effects*** _for more information._

While a spell or ability on the chain is **Resolving**, no other spells or abilities can be finalized on the chain or resolved, including triggered abilities or game effects that would occur as a result of the execution of the spell.
Finish resolving _all effects_ of a spell before addressing any chain items or Tasks the spell may have added or made outstanding through execution.
Certain **Keywords** on spells are not executable rules text, but instead intrinsic properties of the spell.
These determine inherent properties and behaviors of the spell before being played or while on the chain.
These keywords are:
**Action**

This indicates that, in addition to being able to be played during an **Open State**, this spell may _also_ be played during **Open States** during **Showdowns.** _See_ ***rule 806. Action*** _for more information._
**Reaction**
Grants all cases and rules of **Action.**
In addition to all prior cases, may _also_ be played during **all forms of Closed State.**

This will result in this spell resolving before spells and abilities that are already on the chain.
_See_ ***rule 813. Reaction*** _for more information._
**Runes**
**Rune** is a **Card Type.**
A **Rune** is not a **Main Deck** card.
This means, despite remaining on **the Board** until **Recycled** or otherwise removed from the board, it is _not_ a **Permanent.**
**Runes** are kept in the **Rune Deck.**

Exactly 12 **Rune** cards chosen during Deck Construction.
_See_ ***rule 103.3. Rune Deck*** _for more information._
When a **Rune** is **Recycled** it is returned to the **Rune Deck**, not the **Main Deck.** _See_ ***rule 416. Recycle*** _for more information._
**Runes** produce the resources needed to pay costs.
**Runes** produce **Energy** and **Power.**
**Energy** is used to pay numeric **Energy costs.**
**Energy** has no Domain.
**Energy** has no type.
**Power** is used to pay Domain-associated **Power Costs.**
**Power** has a Domain.
**Power**'s Domain usually corresponds to the Domain of the **Rune** that produced it.
Some **Power** is **Universal** and can be used to pay for costs of any Domain.
**Basic** **Runes**

There are six **Basic Runes**, each with a Domain corresponding to its name:
Fury Rune
Calm Rune
Mind Rune
Body Rune
Chaos Rune
Order Rune
A **Basic Rune** always has the following two **Abilities**:
[E]: [Reaction] — Add [1].
Recycle this: [Reaction] — Add [C].
The **Power** added this way corresponds to the **Domain** of the **Rune** that is being **Recycled.**
Rune Pools
The **Rune Pool** is a conceptual collection of a player's available **Energy** and **Power** available to pay **Costs.**
When a card adds **Energy** or **Power,** it is added to the controlling player's **Rune Pool**.
Players must add **Energy** and **Power** to their **Rune Pool** in order to be able to spend it to play cards or pay for **Abilities** with costs.

**Energy** and **Power** do not have a physical marker or tracker, although players may wish to use a physical tracker if they are retaining unspent **Energy** and **Power** over the course of their turn.
Every player's **Rune Pool** empties at the start of each player's Main Phase and the end of each player's turn.
Any unspent **Energy** or **Power** are lost.
All **Abilities** that include the action "**Add**" are abilities that are adding **Energy** or **Power** to the **Rune Pool.** _See_ ***rule 429. Add*** _for more information._
**Battlefields**
**Battlefields** are **Game Objects.**
**Battlefields** are **Owned** by a player.
**Battlefields** are not shuffled into Decks at the start of a game.
**Battlefields** are not played during the course of regular play.
**Battlefields** are established at the start of a game and remain in place for the duration of regular play.
**Battlefields** cannot be **Killed** during the course of regular play.
**Battlefields** cannot be **Moved.**
**Battlefields** are **Locations.**
**Battlefields** can be **Origins** of moves.
**Battlefields** can be **Destinations** of moves.
Any number of **Units** can be present at a **Battlefield.**
**Battlefields** can be targeted by spells or game effects.

**Battlefields** can have **Passive Abilities.**
_See_ ***rule 363. Passive Abilities*** _for more information._

**Battlefields** can have **Triggered Abilities.**
_See_ ***rule 382. Triggered Abilities*** _for more information._

**Battlefields** can have **Activated Abilities.**
_See_ ***rule 376. Activated Abilities*** _for more information._
**Battlefields** can be referenced in different states in card text:
**Battlefields** can be “occupied.” This means they have a **Unit** present.
**Battlefields** can be “uncontrolled.” This means no player controls them.
**Battlefields** can be “open.” This means they are unoccupied and uncontrolled.
**Battlefields** are not **Permanents.**
The number of **Battlefields** on **the Board** is determined by the **Mode of Play.**
**Legends**
**Legends** are **Game Objects.**
**Legends** are **Owned** by a player.
**Legends** are not shuffled into Decks at the start of a game.
**Legends** are not played during the course of regular play.
**Legends** are established at the start of a game, and remain in place for the duration of regular play.
**Legends** cannot be **Killed** during the course of regular play.
**Legends** cannot be **Moved.**
**Legends** can be targeted by spells or game effects.

**Legends** can have **Passive Abilities.**
_See_ ***rule 363. Passive Abilities*** _for more information._

**Legends** can have **Triggered Abilities.**
_See_ ***rule 382. Triggered Abilities*** _for more information._

**Legends** can have **Activated Abilities.**
_See_ ***rule 376. Activated Abilities*** _for more information._
**Legends** are not **Permanents.**
**Legends** may have one or more **Domains.**
The **Champion Legend** determines the **Domain Identity** of cards its owner can include. _See_ ***rule 101. Deck Construction*** _for more information._
**Multiple Types**
**Game Objects** can have multiple types.
A **Game Object** that is more than one type has the properties of all of their types, except where they are mutually exclusive.

Regardless of a **Game Objects’** other types, there are some properties that are true of **Game** **Objects** of certain types. These properties take precedence when properties are mutually exclusive.
If a **Game Object** is a **Unit,** regardless of its other types, it has Might, can have damage marked on it, enters exhausted, and can be played to any valid location.
It will not be recalled to base in step 5 of a cleanup.
If a **Game Object** is a **Rune,** regardless of its other types, it will be recycled to the **Rune Deck.**
A **Game Object** that is more than one type maintains all of the permissions of all types.
If a **Game Object** is a **Unit,** it has the standard move inherent ability.
It can move and become located at a Battlefield.

A **Game Object** that is more than one type can be affected by Game Effects that modify or interact with any of its types.
_Example:_ A unit that is also a gear can be affected by spells and abilities like the Ruination or Thermo Beam that say “Kill all units,” or “Kill all gear.” A unit that is also a gear can be targeted by spells and abilities like Vengeance or Rocket Barrage that say “Kill a unit,” or “Kill a gear.”
**Tokens**
**Tokens** are **Game Objects** created by spells and abilities during play.
Tokens can be represented by anything. Printed tokens are included in **Riftbound** booster packs, but they are not required to play a token.

A token's **controller** is the controller of the spell or ability that created it, unless the token's type innately determines control or that spell or ability specifies that a different player is the token's controller.
A token's **owner** is the player who controlled the effect that created it.
The effect that creates a token may specify the conditions or circumstances under which it enters the board. These stipulations may alter the usual steps for playing a card if the token is played.
The effect may state that the token enters ready or exhausted, if that state is contrary to the default for the token's type.
The effect may restrict the location to which the token may be played.
The effect may grant temporary abilities or modifications to the token.
Tokens are not cards.
“Token” is an intrinsic category of **Game Objects,** in the same way “card” is.
Token **Game Objects** cannot lose their token nature by any means.
Card **Game Objects** cannot become tokens by any means.
Tokens have some properties in common with cards.

Tokens can be **played** by their owner if their card type is played, following all the applicable steps for playing a card plus any restrictions or modifications from the effect that created the token.
_See_ ***rule 349. Playing Cards*** _for more information._
Token units have a **Might**.
Tokens may have one or more **tags**.

Tokens have a **type**. They follow all rules for their type unless otherwise specified. _Example:_ A token unit is a unit. It enters exhausted, can take the standard move action, deals damage equal to its Might in combat, is destroyed if it takes damage equal to or greater than its might, can be chosen or otherwise affected by spells or abilities that choose or affect units, etc.

Tokens inherit the recycle destination of their type.
_Example:_ Token runes will be recycled to the Rune Deck. Token units, gear, and spells will be recycled to the Main Deck. Token battlefields and legends can’t be recycled.
Tokens differ from cards in some ways.
Tokens do not have **costs**.
Although tokens do not have costs, their cost is treated as being 0 for all purposes.

Tokens can have costs appended to them via applied Layer effects. _Example:_ Deceiver’s conquer effect creates a 0 [M] Reflection unit token and applies a copy effect to that token. The copy effect will append all copyable traits, including the cost of the unit to be copied. This appends a cost to the Reflection token.
Tokens do not have **domains**.

Tokens can have domains appended to them via applied Layer effects. _Example:_ A card reads in part “As you play this, pick a domain. All units and gear are that domain.” If that card is in play, any unit and gear tokens will have the chosen domain appended to them.
Tokens are **Created** on the board or the Chain and cannot exist elsewhere.
If a token is put into any **Non-Board Zone** besides the chain, it ceases to exist immediately after moving to its new zone.
The spell or ability that **Creates** a token specifies some of its characteristics. It may have other characteristics, as listed below.
A **1 [M] Recruit token** is a domainless unit token with 1 Might and the Recruit tag.

A **3 [M] Sprite token with Temporary** is a domainless unit token with 3 Might, the Fae tag, and the **Temporary** keyword.
_See_ ***rule 816. Temporary*** _for more information._
A **2 [M] Sand Soldier token** is a domainless unit token with 2 Might and the Shurima tag.
A **3 [M]** **Mech token** is a domainless unit token with 3 Might and the Mech tag.
A **Gold gear token** is a domainless gear token with “[Reaction][>] Kill this, [E]: [Add] [A].”
A **0 [M] Reflection token** is a domainless unit token with 0 Might.
A **1 [M] Bird token** is a domainless unit token with 1 Might, the Bird tag, and the **Deflect** keyword. _See_ ***rule 809. Deflect*** _for more information._

A **Brush battlefield token** is a domainless battlefield token with “Bird, Cat, Dog, Poro, and Ivern units here have +1 [M]” and “When you score here, you may replace this with the battlefield it replaced.”
The **Baron Pit battlefield token** is a domainless battlefield token with “Units can move here from anywhere.”
A **1 [M] Tentacle token** is a domainless unit token with 1 Might and the Bilgewater tag.

A **0 [M] Shadow Clone token** is a domainless unit token with 0 Might and “When I attack, you may banish a unit from your trash. If you do, give me [Assault 4] this turn.”
_See_ ***rule 807. Assault*** _for more information._
**Control**
**Control** is the concept of a player having influence of a **Game Object** and applies differently to different card types.
**Battlefields**
**Control** is established over **Battlefields** through the course of play.
**Control** is a binary state for **Battlefields** and an **Identifier** for players.
A **Battlefield** is **Controlled** or **Uncontrolled.**
A **Battlefield** is **Controlled** by a specific player or **Controlled** by no one.
**Control** can be **Contested** through the course of play.

**Contested** is a temporary status applied to the battlefield when a **Unit** controlled by a **Player** who does not currently **Control** that **Battlefield** **Moves** or otherwise becomes present there.

**Units** moving to or being played to a battlefield apply **Contested** status if that battlefield is not already **Contested** and that **Unit**’s controller does not already control that battlefield.
Once a **Showdown** or **Combat** begins at a **Battlefield,** it remains **Contested** until **Control** is established or re-established.

If a player who applied **Contested** status to a **Battlefield** controls no **Units** there and there is no ongoing Showdown or Combat at that **Battlefield,** then **Contested** status is removed in the following Cleanup.
The state of a **Battlefield** being **Contested** is used to determine when **Combat** should occur and when a **Non-Combat Showdown** should occur.
At this time **Game Effects** cannot reference this status.
**Control** is established by having **Units** at a **Battlefield** at the end of a Showdown or Combat after applying the contested status.
If a player controls **Units** at a **Battlefield,** outside of **Combat,** they maintain **Control** of that **Battlefield** for as long as they have **Units** at that **Battlefield.**
While a **Combat** or **Showdown** is ongoing at a **Battlefield**, **Control** of that **Battlefield** cannot change until instructed by steps of the **Combat** or **Showdown**.

If a player has no **Units** at a **Battlefield** and the turn is in an **Open** state, they lose **Control** of that **Battlefield** in the following cleanup unless there is a **Combat** or **Showdown** ongoing there.
**Control** is a constant state.
**Control** of a **Battlefield** determines **Control** of its **Abilities.**

While a **Battlefield** is **Controlled**, its **Controller** controls its **Abilities** unless that ability indicates another player does. The controlling player takes responsibility for adding them to the **Chain** if applicable, and makes all choices required by them unless otherwise specified.

While a **Battlefield** is **Uncontrolled**, its **Abilities** are also **Uncontrolled** unless that ability indicates a player controls it. If there is no such indication, the **Turn Player** takes responsibility for adding them to the **Chain** if applicable, makes all choices required by them unless otherwise specified, and is treated as their **Controller** if any game rule or effect requires one.
_Example:_ The Arena’s Greatest is a battlefield that reads “At the start of each player's first Beginning Phase, that player gains 1 point.” This ability will usually trigger while the battlefield has no controller. If it does, the Turn Player goes through the steps of adding the ability to the chain and receives priority after doing so, exactly as if they controlled the ability.

If an **Ability** of a **Battlefield** indicates that a specific player makes a choice, that player is the Ability’s controller. They take responsibility for adding it to the chain if applicable and make all choices required by the ability. They and only they control the ability, regardless of who controls the **Battlefield.**
_Example:_ Abandoned Hall is a battlefield that reads “When a player plays a spell, they may give a unit they control here +1 might this turn.” The ability indicates that the player who played the spell makes the choice, so that player takes responsibility for putting the ability on the chain and makes all choices. They control the triggered ability.

“You” in a battlefield’s abilities refers to the battlefield’s **Controller**, as does the implied “you” in instructions that don’t specify a player like “draw 1.” If the battlefield has no **Controller**, “you” refers to no one, and all such instructions are ignored.
**Everything Else**
When a player **Plays, Hides,** or **Creates** a **Card** or other **Game Object**, they are established as that **Game Object's** **Controller.**
For **Spells**, they are the **Spell's Controller.**
That player chooses targets.
That player chooses modes.
That player pays costs.
For **Permanents** and **Runes**, when they **Enter the Board**, that player is assigned as that **Game** **Object's** **Controller.**
That player may make decisions about the **Game Object's Inherent Abilities.**
That player may make decisions about the **Game Object's Unique Abilities.**
That player may make decisions about any game effects or decisions necessary while the card is being played.
That player may make decisions about any game effects created from "When you play me" effects of **Permanents.**
For **Abilities**, they are the **Ability’s Controller.**
By default, the **Controller** of an **Ability’s Source** is the **Controller** of that **Ability.**
If an **Ability’s Source** is located in a **non-Board Zone**, the **Ability’s** **Controller** is the **Owner** of the **Source.**
Changes to **Control** of an **Ability’s Source** do not change **Control** of that **Ability.**
That player chooses targets.
That player chooses modes.
That player pays costs.
When a game effect or rules text refers to the **Controller** of a specific object, it can be referring to either context interchangeably.
The method of assignment of control is different, but the status of **Control** is the same across all **Game Objects.**
**Winning**
Players win _Riftbound_ games primarily through gaining **points.**
Players can gain points a number of ways:

**Holding** a **Battlefield.**
_See_ ***rule 467. Scoring*** _for more information._

**Conquering** a **Battlefield.**
_See_ ***rule 467. Scoring*** _for more information._
**Spells, Triggered Abilities** and **Activated Abilities** that instruct them to gain one or more points.

When an opponent **Burns Out** and picks that player to gain 1 point.
_See_ ***rule 431. Burn Out*** _for more information._

A player wins the game if, in a cleanup, they have points greater than or equal to the **Victory Score** and more points than any other player.
_See_ ***rule 318. Cleanups*** _for more information._
If more than one player has points greater than or equal to the **Victory Score,** whichever player has more points wins.
If those players have the same number of points, play continues until one player has more points in a cleanup.
The **Victory Score** is 8 points by default.

Some game modes or card effects may alter the Victory Score.
_See_ ***rule 481. Modes of Play*** _for more information._
Players cannot have less than 0 points.
If a player would lose 1 or more points while they have 0 points, nothing occurs.
Any effects that trigger on a player losing points do not trigger.
A player also wins the game if an effect instructs them to do so, or if they are the only player remaining in the game.
If an effect instructs a player to lose the game, that player is immediately removed from the game. _See_ ***rule 649. Conceding*** _for more information about being removed from the game._
When a player wins the game, the game ends.
**Locations**
**Locations** are the **Game Objects** and **Zones** where permanents and runes may be located.
**Locations** include the **Battlefields** and the **Bases.**
A permanent’s **Location** is a characteristic of that permanent that may be referenced by **Game** **Effects.**
A **Game Object** that comprises a **Location** is also a **Game Object** at that location.
A **Battlefield** is both a location and also a **Game Object** at that location.

**Locations** can be **Destinations** and **Origins** for movement.
_See_ ***rule 445. Movement*** _for more information._
**Costs**
Some **Game Effects** will instruct players to pay resources, spend counters or XP, or perform game actions as a **Cost** in order to perform a linked **Effect.**
**Costs** must be paid in order to get their linked **Effect.**
Paying a **Cost** requires a player to perform the appropriate game action: pay resources, spend counters or XP, kill units, etc.
The **Cost** is still considered paid even if the associated game action is replaced.
If the game action associated with a **Cost** is impossible for any reason such that a player cannot perform it, then they cannot pay the **Cost** and they will not execute the linked **Effect.**
**Costs** paid in this way might be:
**Base Costs:** These costs are the most fundamental costs that must be paid to finalize a spell or ability.
On cards, the **Base Cost** is found in the upper left corner of the card.
On activated abilities, the **Base Cost** is the resource or instruction written before the “:”.
**Additional Costs:** These **Costs** are in addition to the base cost of the spell or ability and will include “as an additional cost.”
**Additional Costs** must be paid to finalize the spell or ability, in addition to the base cost.
**Costs within Instructions:** These **Costs** are part of the resolution of a spell or ability, and will take the form “[do X] to [do Y].”

When **Costs within Instructions** appear as the first part of the effect in triggered abilities, the cost is taken to be the base cost of the triggered ability. It must be paid to finalize the triggered ability.
_Example:_ Overzealous Fan is a unit that reads “When I defend, you may kill me to move an attacking unit to its base.” In order to finalize the ability to the chain, its controller must kill Overzealous Fan.

When **Costs within Instructions** appear in spells, activated abilities, or in later parts of triggered abilities, the cost must be paid to get the effect during resolution of the spell or ability.
_Example:_ Bullet Time is a spell that reads “Pay any amount of [A] to deal that much damage to all enemy units at a battlefield.” As the spell resolves, its controller pays any amount of [A] as a cost, and deals that much damage to all units at a battlefield as the effect.
_Example:_ Ancient Henge is a gear that reads “[Reaction][>] [E]: Pay any amount of Energy to Add that much [A].” As the ability resolves, its controller pays any amount of Energy as a cost, and Adds that much [A] as the effect.
_Example:_ Insightful Investigator is a unit that reads “When you play me, choose an opponent. They reveal their hand. You may pay 2 XP to choose a card from their hand. If you do, they discard that card and draw 1.” As the ability resolves, its controller may pay 2 XP as a cost, and chooses a card from that player’s hand as the effect.

**Applied Costs:** These **Costs** are applied to one or more Game Actions, and typically take the form of a passive ability with a **Cost within Instructions** preceded by “must.”
_Example:_ Mageseeker Investigator reads in part “Opponents must pay [A] for each unit beyond the first to move multiple units to my battlefield at the same time.” This is an applied cost.
The associated Game Action is the linked **Effect** for the **Cost.**
**Applied Costs** are paid as the Game Action is performed. They do not use the chain and cannot be reacted to.

Abilities that Add resources with the Reaction keyword can be activated at any time when costs need to be paid.
_See_ ***rule 429. Add*** _for more information._
If a player can’t pay or chooses not to pay the **Applied Cost**, they cannot perform the associated Game Action.

An instruction that requires a player to pay resources or spend counters or XP that does not also have a linked **Effect,** is not a **Cost.**
_Example:_ A card effect reads, “When I attack, you may pay [4][C]. If you do, kill a unit here.” Paying [4][C] in this way is not a cost of the ability, base or otherwise, but a game action being performed by a player. The later instruction checks whether the game action was performed, not whether a cost was paid—if paying [4][C] was replaced for any reason, the later linked instruction will not execute.

Effects that need to determine a card’s cost for any purpose always use its printed or copied cost, even if that cost is increased, decreased, or ignored as the card is played.
_Example:_ Sky Splitter is a spell that costs [8] and says in part “This spell's Energy cost is reduced by the highest Might among units you control.” Lux, Illuminated is a unit that says “When you play a spell that costs 5 or more, give me +3 [M] this turn.” If a player who controls Lux plays Sky Splitter, Lux’s ability will trigger, regardless of how much was actually paid for Sky Splitter.
_Example:_ Defy is a spell which reads “Counter a spell that costs no more than [4] and no more than [A].” Rocket Barrage is a spell that costs [4][C] and has a Repeat cost of [4][C]. Rocket Barrage is a legal target for Defy even if Rocket Barrage’s Repeat cost is paid, because Defy only checks the printed or copied cost of its target.
_Example:_ Atakhan is a unit that reads in part “You may kill a friendly unit as an additional cost to play me. If you do, I cost [1] less for each Energy it costs and [Y] less for each Power it costs.” If a player plays Atakhan and chooses to kill a Reflection token that is currently a copy of Noxus Hopeful, Atakhan will cost [4] less.

Effects that need to determine an ability’s cost for any purpose use its base cost, ignoring any alterations to that base cost unless otherwise specified.
_Example:_ A card reads “When you play an activated ability with Energy cost [7] or more, you may exhaust me to ready up to 2 runes.” A gear has an activated ability that reads “[12], Banish this: Choose an opponent. Banish all units they control. This ability costs [1] less for each point the chosen opponent has.” The ability of the first card will trigger when the activated ability is played, even if the chosen opponent has 6 or more points. _Example:_ An equipment has an ability that reads “Equip [4][A]. This ability costs [1] less for each occupied enemy battlefield.” If its controller plays a unit with Weaponmaster, the Weaponmaster trigger will refer to the cost including the alterations. This is because Weaponmaster specifies that it includes modifications to the cost.
**Playing the Game**
**The Turn**
Play continues cyclically until one player wins.
The phases of a turn are rigid, but the actions taken during those steps can be done in any order, unless otherwise specified.
**Game Actions** of any nature are performed one at a time and are executed completely.
**Game Actions** cannot be performed simultaneously for any reason.

If one or more game actions, game effects, or **Triggered Abilities** are activated simultaneously, then **Turn Order** is referenced to organize the sequence of actions, starting with the current **Turn Player.**
_See_ ***rule 382. Triggered Abilities*** _for more information._
The **Turn Player** is the player taking the current turn.

When there are no items on the **Chain** and the **Turn Player** cannot or chooses not to perform any **Discretionary Actions**, the current phase or step of the turn ends and the next phase, step, or turn begins.
The **Turn Player** changes when the current **Turn Player** reaches the **End** of all of the **Phases** of their **Turn.**
States of the Turn
At any given time, the turn is in either a **Neutral State** or a **Showdown State.**
If a **Showdown** or **Combat** is in progress, the turn is in a **Showdown State.**
Only cards and abilities with the **Action** or **Reaction** keywords can be played or activated in a **Showdown State.**
If no **Showdown** or **Combat** is in progress, the turn is in a **Neutral State.**
At any given time, the turn is in either an **Open State** or a **Closed State.**
If a **Chain** exists, the turn is in a **Closed State.**
Only cards and abilities with the **Reaction** keyword can be played or activated in a **Closed** **State.**
If no **Chain** exists, the turn is in an **Open State.**
These descriptions can be combined, such that the turn is always in one of these four states:
**Neutral Open:** There is no **Showdown** or **Combat** in progress and no **Chain** exists.
By default, cards can be played and abilities activated only when a player has priority on their turn in a **Neutral Open** state.
**Neutral Closed:** There is no **Showdown** or **Combat** in progress and a **Chain** exists.
**Showdown Open:** A **Showdown** or **Combat** is in progress and no **Chain** exists.
**Showdown Closed:** A **Showdown** or **Combat** is in progress and a **Chain** exists.
Priority and Focus
At any given time, up to one player has **Priority.**

**Priority** is the singular exclusive right to take **Discretionary Actions.**
_See_ ***rule 410.1. Discretionary Actions*** _for more information._
The player with **Priority** can take appropriately timed **Discretionary Actions.**
If no player has **Priority**, no player can take **Discretionary Actions.**
Players can always take and make choices for **Limited Actions** when instructed, regardless of **Priority.**
A player receives **Priority** at the following times:
When the turn is in a **Neutral Open State** during their **Main Phase.**
When the turn is in a **Showdown State** and they gain **Focus.**
When the turn is in a **Closed State,** all pending chain items finish being finalized, and they control the next item on the **Chain.**
When the turn is in a **Closed State**, they are the next **Player** in **Turn Order**, and the player with **Priority** passes.
When a player is granted **Priority,** it is either created if no player has it or taken from the player with **Priority.**
At any given time, up to one player has **Focus.**

**Focus** is the permission to take appropriately timed **Discretionary Actions** when the turn is in a **Showdown Open State.**
_See_ ***rule 307. States of the Turn*** _for more information._

The player with **Focus** must obey any additional restrictions on which **Discretionary Actions** may be performed.
_Example:_ A player with **Focus** may not play spells or activate abilities that don't have the **Action** or **Reaction** keywords.
A player who gains **Focus** also gains **Priority.**
A player who passes **Priority** retains **Focus.**
A player may not make discretionary actions with **Focus** unless they also possess **Priority.**
If the turn is in a **Neutral State**, no player has **Focus.**
Phases of the Turn
**Start of Turn**
_Awaken Phase_
The following **Task** becomes **Outstanding**:
1. The **Turn Player** readies all **Game Objects** they control that are able to be readied. _See_ ***rule 415. Ready*** _for more information._
_Beginning Phase_
Beginning Step
At the start of Beginning Phase game effects take place.
Scoring Step
The following **Task** becomes **Outstanding**:

1. The **Turn Player** **Holds** all **Battlefields** they **Control**.
_See_ ***rule 467. Scoring*** _for more information._

_Reminder: In_ ***Modes of Play*** _with_ ***Teams***_,_ ***Battlefields*** _controlled by a Teammate of_ _the_ ***Turn Player*** _during this step are_ ***disqualified*** _from being scored this turn by_ _the_ ***Turn Player.***
_Channel Phase_
The following **Task** becomes **Outstanding**:

1. The **Turn Player** channels 2 runes from their **Rune Deck.**
_See_ ***rule 430. Channel*** _for more information._
If there are fewer than 2 runes in the **Rune Deck,** they channel as many as possible.
_Draw Phase_
The following **Task** becomes **Outstanding**:
1. The **Turn Player** draws 1.

If there are no cards remaining in their **Main Deck** to draw, the **Turn Player** has been **Burned Out**.
_See_ ***rule 431. Burn Out*** _for more information._
After completing the **Burn Out** the **Turn Player** still **Draws 1.**
**Main Phase**
When all steps of the **Start of Turn** have been completed, the **Main Phase** begins.
The following **Tasks** become **Outstanding** in the specified order:

1. Each player's **Rune Pool** empties. Any unspent **Energy** and **Power** are lost.
_See_ ***rule 165. Rune Pools*** _for more information._
2. At the start of Main Phase game effects take place.
The **Main Phase** has no defined structure.

A player may take any number of **Discretionary Actions** they are able to perform during this phase.
_See_ ***rule 410.1. Discretionary Actions*** _for more information._

This is denoted as a **Neutral** **Open State**, and only the **Turn Player** has the ability to play spells or activate abilities.
_See_ ***rule 307. States of the Turn*** _for more information._

In **Modes of Play** with teammates, the **Turn Player's** teammates may play spells and activate abilities, including ones without **Action** or **Reaction.** They can only do so when the **Turn Player** invites them to do so with their own **Priority.**
As a result of a player taking **Discretionary Actions**, one or more structured phases may occur.
**Combat**
A **Combat** occurs as a result of **Units** controlled by opposing players being present at the same **Battlefield**.
This could be the result of a **Standard Move** Intrinsic Ability, a **Spell**, or other **Game Effect.**
The source effect does not change the structure or flow of **Combat** once initiated.

A **Combat** can only occur between two players.
_See_ ***rule 459. Combat*** _for more information._

Play proceeds following the steps of combat.
_See_ ***rule 463. The Steps of Combat*** _for more information._
Combat will also include a **Showdown.**
**Showdowns**
A **Showdown** occurs when a **Combat** occurs.
Showdowns that occur as a result of Combat are a **Sub-Phase** of **Combat.**
A **Showdown** is marked as **Staged** at a **Battlefield** in the cleanup after the **Contested** status is applied to that **Battlefield.**
Showdowns that occur as a result of a player moving to an empty **Battlefield** are a stand-alone **Phase** and do not create a **Combat.**

These **Showdowns** are called **Non-Combat Showdowns**. During the proceedings of a **Non-Combat Showdown**, units controlled by a different player may become present at the Battlefield where the **Showdown** is ongoing. This will cause the **Showdown** to become a **Combat Showdown** in the following cleanup.

A **Showdown** is a structured **Window of Opportunity** where **Players** may play cards and activate abilities with **Action** or **Reaction.**
_See_ ***rule 341. Showdowns*** _for more information._
When a player has no more **Discretionary Actions** they wish to execute, they must indicate they are ending their turn.
This ends the **Main Phase.**
Play proceeds to the **Ending Phase.**
**Ending Phase**
Ending Step
At the end of the turn **Game Effects** take place.
Specific game effects and abilities will reference this timing and phase as necessary.
Expiration Step

Invoke an Ending **Special Cleanup.**
_See_ ***rule 318. Cleanups*** _for more information._
Insert “3c. **Heal** all **Units**.”
Insert “3d. All ‘this turn’ effects expire simultaneously.”
Insert “3e. Each player’s **Rune Pool** empties. Any unspent **Energy** and **Power** are lost.”
The following **Task** becomes **Outstanding**:
1. If any items underwent the FEPR process, return to the start of the Expiration Step
The next player with their **Turn** queued becomes the **Turn Player.**
**Cleanups**
A **Cleanup** will be made an **Outstanding Task** at the following times:
After the game transitions to or from an **Open** or **Closed** state
After the game transitions between **Phases,** unless specified otherwise
After a **Pending Item** is added to the **Chain**
After a **Pending Item** becomes a **Finalized Item** on the **Chain**
After a **Chain Item** is removed from the **Chain** for any reason
After any number of **Game Objects** enter or leave the Board
After the status of any number of **Game Objects** changes for any reason
After a **Move** is completed
While a **Cleanup** is occurring, **Chain Items** cannot be **Finalized** or **Resolved.**
New **Pending Items** can be added, but **Finalized Items** cannot be executed and **Priority** and **Focus** are not passed or awarded.
Similarly, while **Chain Items** are **Resolving**, a **Cleanup** cannot occur.
If an event occurs during the **Resolution** of a **Chain Item** that qualifies for a **Cleanup**, that **Cleanup** will be made an **Outstanding Task.**

If an event occurs during a **Cleanup** that qualifies for a **Cleanup,** another **Cleanup** will occur immediately after the first completes, repeating until a **Cleanup** occurs with no new change in the game’s state.
These new **Cleanups** are themselves **Outstanding Tasks.**
When a **Cleanup** occurs, the following **Tasks** become **Outstanding** in the order described:
1. If a player has points greater than or equal to the **Victory Score,** and more points than any opponent, that player wins.
2. Assign or Remove the **Attacker** or **Defender** designation from **Units** as needed if there is a **Combat** in progress
If there are **Units** present at the **Battlefield** the **Combat** is taking place at, but do not have a designation, they gain the same designation as their **Controller** now

If there are **Units** present at the **Battlefield** the **Combat** is taking place at, but have the opposite designation of their controller, they lose that designation, and gain the same designation as their controller now
